projects.security.title: site.hopen
projects.security.title.text
site.hclose
site.popen
The use of Slackware Linux as the underlying platform imposed additional integration complexity. Many components, supporting libraries,
and authentication dependencies required custom compilation, patching, or manual integration in order to achieve a stable and interoperable
security architecture. Although non-trivial, this process provided fine-grained control over system behaviour, dependency management, and
system integration. It also facilitated a minimalist operating system footprint, permitting more aggressive oversubscription of available
hardware resources.
site.pclose
site.br
site.popen
Building critical components from source also permitted optimisation for target hardware and workload characteristics. Numerical and
scientific libraries such as BLAS and LAPACK are frequently distributed in broadly compatible but conservatively optimised forms. Tailored
builds allowed exploitation of architecture-specific capabilities while retaining tighter control over dependency management and interoperability.
site.pclose
site.br
projects.security.overview.title: site.br
site.hopen
Overview of Security Architecture
site.hclose
site.br
projects.security.overview.1: Security is Fundamental, never an afterthought, never a 'nice to have'. An admin who is the custodian of others' data must be responsible for
protecting it in the same way that they would protect their own valuable assets.
projects.security.overview.2: One would not install cheap locks on one's dwelling — the same diligence in the selection of software ought to be observed.
projects.security.overview.3: Security checklists are great in terms of reducing the potential for an oversight but it is important to grasp the fundamentals in security
design. If one sets a tenon type lock in a softwood frame, rather than steel, then one ought not, perhaps, to be surprised if an intruder breaks
the frame to gain entry. The metaphor applies equally to computing infrastructure.
projects.security.overview.4: Security must be complementary, and largely invisible; not an unwieldy imposition.
projects.security.overview.5: Corporate buzzwords alone do not make systems secure. Although ideas such as "Essential Eight", "Zero Trust", and similar frameworks are
gaining momentum throughout the Noosphere, they are not substitutes for disciplined system design, sound operational practice, and rigorous
implementation. The danger posed by 'buzzword governance' is that genuine rigour may be displaced by a kind of security theatre built around
procedural tokenism. For similar reasons, I did not implement the likes of clevis and tang.
projects.security.overview.6: System Security and Operational Security are separate things. Data Integrity and System Availability are as important as other aspects of Operating
projects.security.overview.7: The constraints that needed to be met for my reference architecture were that the system needed to be robust, deployable at scale, built upon mature,
well-understood technologies broadly compatible with target software (Hadoop, Zookeeper, Apache-drill), accessible to both interactive user logins
and non-interactive service accounts. Authentication and Authorisation were critical requirements. These concerns now commonly fall under the broader
projects.security.overview.8: All physical disk partitions, with the exception of those hosting UEFI boot filesystems are encrypted with LUKS. The disks are first written with
cryptographically random data. LVM Volume Groups are created over the LUKS-encrypted disks. Keys are stored on USB sticks. The initial intent was
to resin-seal them inside some 2" x 1/4"x.s. steel tube with a couple of tangs welded on, to screw the container down, then drill out the phillips-heads
to make removal less trivial. On re-evaluating my use-case, the sensitivity of my data, and the probability of physical intrusion the measures
projects.security.components.title: site.hopen
Key Components
site.hclose
projects.security.components.1: OpenLDAP — User Database and backing store for Kerberos.
projects.security.components.2: MIT Kerberos 5 — Principal and Ticket Management.
projects.security.components.3: Time Synchronisation Services — Critical for Kerberos authentication and ticket validity.
projects.security.components.4: DHCP and BIND9 Name Services — Simplifies network management and provides reliable forward and reverse name resolution
required for stable LDAP and Kerberos operation.
projects.security.components.5: NFSv4 — Shared, ACL-enabled, read-only network filesystem used for distribution of keytabs and SSL certificates.
projects.security.components.6: Internal Certificate Authority and Intermediate CA infrastructure supporting SSL/TLS-secured client-server communications.
projects.security.components.7: Minimal PAM configuration supporting LDAP and Kerberos authentication integration.
projects.security.components.8: NSLCD and NSCD — Credential and directory caching daemons used to reduce authentication and lookup overhead.
projects.security.components.9: CyrusSASL — Library to facilitate authentication mechanisms, particularly GSSAPI.

Turnip - Disk Partitioning and Data Layout
site.hclose
projects.acl.title: site.hopen
XFS and NFSv4 Access Control Lists.
site.hclose
projects.acl.1: My physical systems are constrained by their hardware configurations and available resources. These limitations necessarily influenced the scale and composition
A secure means of distributing keys and keytabs across the infrastructure.
Simple, high-level ownership (root) and ACL-based access — XFS ACLs for local access, and NFSv4 ACLs for remote access.
The whole of the Identity Management infrastructure is configured using a few scripts and ldif format files. KRB5 principals were defined using a set of scripts, as
were service ACLs. The obvious evolution of this would be an ansible approach, however, the priorities lie elsewhere.
projects.opsec.title: site.hopen
Operational Security.
site.hclose
projects.opsec.1: My physical systems are modest. The degree of operational security attainable is constrained by cost; however, critical data is stored on RAID1
mirrored storage pairs, and full image backups are periodically written to separate offline media.
My laptop has been configured with both a primary root volume and a secondary backup root volume. This permits a full copy of the root filesystem
to be maintained as a hot standby environment.
System upgrades can be applied to the offline root volume prior to deployment.
Limited testing may be performed by chrooting into the offline environment before conducting a full boot test.
In the event that problems manifest after the upgraded system is booted, the system can be trivially failed back to the previous root environment.
Minor issues may often be repaired in an ad hoc fashion without requiring full rollback.
Issues that do not warrant failback, but are not immediately resolvable, can frequently be worked around by chrooting back into the previous root
volume whilst the upgraded environment remains operational.
Additional recovery and maintenance operations are facilitated using tools such as qemu-nbd, permitting offline mounting and manipulation of virtual
machine disk images for repair, migration, and recovery purposes.
Forge, my VM hosting machine, was built on a budget, but with performance in mind as well as data security to the extent possible.
